Bitcoin Student Research Prize — BSRP 2026  ·  v1.0 Winner Georgia Institute of Technology
BSRP 2026 — First Place Winner

Aligning Load Flexibility with
Emissions Reduction:
Empirical Insights from a Multi-Site Study
of Cryptocurrency Data Centers

Veronica M. Paez  ·  Neda Mohammadi  ·  John E. Taylor

School of Civil and Environmental Engineering, Georgia Institute of Technology, Atlanta, GA

Word count: 7,772 Study period: July – September 2023 Facilities: 21 North American CDCs Correspondence: mpz@gatech.edu

The power sector is responsible for 32 percent of global greenhouse gas emissions. Data centers and cryptocurrencies use significant amounts of electricity and contribute to these emissions. Demand-side flexibility of data centers is one possible approach for reducing greenhouse gas emissions from these industries. To explore this, we use novel data collected from the Bitcoin mining industry to investigate the impact of load flexibility on power system decarbonization. Employing engineered metrics to explore curtailment dynamics and emissions alignment, we provide the first empirical analysis of cryptocurrency data centers' capability for reducing greenhouse gas emissions in response to real-time grid signals. Our results highlight the importance of strategically aligning operational behaviors with emissions signals to maximize avoided emissions. These findings offer insights for policymakers and industry stakeholders to enhance load flexibility and meet climate goals in these otherwise energy intensive data centers.

Climate change Cryptocurrency mining Data centers Decarbonization Energy transition Load flexibility
32%
of global GHG emissions from the power sector
21
North American Bitcoin mining facilities studied
0.59
Adjusted R² after stepwise regression (up from 0.19)
210%
improvement in model explanatory power

§1 Introduction

Climate change, exacerbated by fossil fuel combustion releasing greenhouse gases, is warming the atmosphere to unprecedented levels. The power sector significantly contributes to this warming, with 32% of global emissions resulting from electricity generation. Data centers (including data-transmission networks) consume an estimated 2.4–3.3% of global electricity. Bitcoin alone uses an estimated 0.44% of global electricity.

As one step toward mitigating the worst outcomes of climate change, countries aim to decarbonize the electrical grid, transitioning to renewable energy sources. As more variable renewable energy provides power to electrical grids, misalignment between supply and demand becomes more common and a serious threat to power system reliability. Research shows that demand-side flexibility—a load's ability to change its power consumption in response to electricity prices or grid signals—provides a cost-effective approach to integrating renewable energy.

Data centers, with their significant power draw, are strong candidates for load flexibility. A 30MW data center could provide the same services as an optimized 7MWh large-scale storage installation. However, traditional data centers have high-reliability requirements that can limit this. In contrast, cryptocurrency data centers (CDCs) reportedly do not have the same reliability concerns and could be modified to provide the necessary flexibility.

Most existing literature addressing flexible loads has relied heavily on simulations or theoretical frameworks applied only to traditional data centers. Operational data from CDCs, which could validate actual behavior and potential for decarbonization, remain scarce. Our study uses novel, real-world energy data from 21 North American CDCs and demonstrates that they are capable of load flexibility and can, to varying degrees, mitigate their greenhouse-gas emissions.

§2 Background

2.1 Data Centers & Cryptocurrency Mining

Data centers house hundreds to tens of thousands of servers, cooling systems, storage drives, and network devices. The International Energy Agency (IEA) estimates that traditional data centers plus data-transmission networks consumed 500–700 TWh of power in 2023. Projections suggest data centers' total electricity consumption could rise to more than 1,000 TWh.

2.1.2 The Rise of the Cryptocurrency Data Center

CDCs operate distinctly from traditional data centers, as they generate revenue from earning cryptocurrency rewards through computational power. Focusing on Bitcoin, a distributed monetary network, reveals how the Proof-of-Work (PoW) security mechanism consumes energy to secure its public transaction ledger. Although only about 5% of cryptocurrencies use PoW, Bitcoin constitutes roughly 64% of the total cryptocurrency market capitalization.

In Bitcoin's "mining," the first computer to find the winning block receives a reward, which halves approximately every four years (since 2024, this reward is 3.125 Bitcoin). The difficulty adjustment regulates how hard it is to find new blocks. Electricity cost has become a primary concern for Bitcoin data centers. Despite efficiency gains in mining machines, Bitcoin's energy demand increased to an estimated 121.13 TWh in 2023.

2.2 Load Flexibility & Demand-Side Flexibility

Variable power sources like solar and wind often cause supply and demand mismatches on the grid. Demand-side flexibility involves various strategies for power consumers to manage electricity demand, including load flexibility, energy efficiency, demand response programs, or distributed energy resources. Demand-side flexibility can reduce the cost of variable renewable energy and serve as a valuable decarbonization tool.

A review of nine studies across multiple countries indicated that load flexibility accounts for roughly 2–20% of decarbonization capacity scenarios. Data center demand response literature, focusing on medium-sized data centers, suggests that improved market design and economic incentives could enhance data center participation.

2.2.3 Carbon Emission Factors

Previous studies estimating CDC carbon emissions primarily used network-wide top-down approaches, employing country-level average grid energy mixes. However, using country-level or global average emissions has limitations—these averages do not account for temporal changes in carbon intensity or interregional electricity flows. Studies relying on low-resolution annual carbon factors can misrepresent a data center's true environmental impact, underestimating emissions by up to 22% or overestimating by 33%.

Locational marginal emission factors (LMEs) capture the hourly carbon intensity of marginal power plants, offering the granularity needed for real-time emissions quantification for individual facilities.

§3 Materials and Methods

3.1 Research Design and Data

We chose a quantitative and observational framework. We contacted 38 private and public CDC providers and received data from 12 (roughly 32%). Ultimately, our study included data from 21 facilities with known location and capacity. Our participant pool included only Bitcoin mining data centers located in North America.

Data were collected on-site at the data centers using industry-standardized equipment. One facility was located in Quebec, Canada; the remaining were situated across seven states: New York, Georgia, Ohio, Kentucky, North Dakota, North Carolina, and Texas. Thirty-three percent of facilities were in Texas; 24% in Georgia.

We identified near-node locational marginal emission factors using the facility's physical, geographical location, collecting nodal LMEs from RESurety and a sub-regional LME set from WattTime for the Canadian site. Our study window spanned July 1–September 30, 2023.

3.2.1 Feature Engineering

We created and assessed multiple independent variables from energy consumption and LME time series data. We used uptime as a foundational indicator of facility activity—the total hours a CDC operated at full load capacity, excluding hours with reduced power due to suspected grid signals or real-time pricing.

Curtailment Magnitude (Equation 1)

Average percentage reduction in energy consumption relative to baseline during curtailment periods
CM = 100 × (1 − Elowest 25% DR / Enon-DR)

Curtailment Regularity (Equation 2)

Standard deviation of curtailment start times, in hours
CR = σstart times / 3600

Maximum Energy (Equation 3)

Highest hourly energy consumption at a facility over the study window
ME = maxd∈D Ed-max

Normalized Avoided Emissions (Equation 4)

Total avoided emissions divided by facility maximum energy use (tons CO₂/MWh)
NAE = A / Emax

Emissions Ratio (Equation 5)

Unitless ratio of avoided to induced emissions
ER = A / I

LME Variability (Equation 6)

Standard deviation of locational marginal emissions for a facility
LMEV = σLME

Avoided Emissions (Equation 7)

Hourly avoided emissions computation
Aid = (H̄d,false − eid) × LMEFid

3.2.2 Curtailment Detection

These data centers generally operate as a constant energy consumer except when curtailing energy consumption. We used the daily maximum energy consumption as a reasonable approximation for the typical baseline load. Using the Kneedle method, we performed a sensitivity test to determine the facility-derived threshold. The knee point across each facility ranged from 75% to 95%, with the mean at 88% and the median at 90%.

3.2.3 Regression Analysis

Given the linear relationship between energy saved and emissions avoided, we employed a forward-backward selection, stepwise regression modeling approach to assess the relationship between uptime, curtailment dynamics, LME variability, and avoided emissions. Variables were retained only if they improved the model's explanatory power and were statistically significant (p ≤ 0.05).

§4 Results

4.1 Comparative Analysis of Regional Emission Profiles

The geographic distribution of the data centers spans the central and eastern portion of North America. When normalized by maximum energy consumption (normalized avoided emissions; tons CO₂/MWh), the facilities in Texas exhibited similar emission profiles. Facilities in Georgia had the widest range of normalized avoided emissions, including a facility that avoided the most emissions per megawatt-hour in our study (Facility 18). Facilities in Kentucky, North Carolina, and Quebec all showed minimal emission savings, suggesting they are among the least flexible in our study.

4.2 Regression Analysis and Performance Categories

The baseline regression reveals that uptime explained only about 19% of the total variance (adjusted R² = 0.19; p < 0.05), showing that simply powering down is not a strong predictor for the total absolute emissions avoided from load flexibility. Uptime had a negative effect on avoided emissions (β = −197.93, SE = 69.07, t = −2.86, p = 0.01).

The facilities fell within three of the four possible performance categories. The High Uptime & Low Avoided Emissions group included 6 facilities. The Low Uptime & Low Avoided Emissions group included 9 facilities. The Low Uptime & High Avoided Emissions group included 6 facilities. Theoretically, a fourth category (High Uptime & High Avoided Emissions) was possible, but none of the facilities aligned to this group.

The forward-backward stepwise regression found two statistically significant features (p < 0.05) in addition to uptime. Maximum energy had a significant positive association with avoided emissions (β = 73.24, SE = 20.09, t = 3.65, p = 0.002). LME variability also had a significant positive effect (β = 43,250, SE = 17,000, t = 2.54, p = 0.021). The combined adjusted R² increased significantly from 0.19 to 0.59—a 210% improvement.

4.3 Case Studies

We examined three facilities representing each performance category to better understand their operational behaviors.

Facility Uptime (%) Avoided Emissions (tons CO₂) Induced Emissions (tons CO₂) Max Energy (MWh) Norm. Avoided (tons CO₂/MWh)
1097.55262.17110,471.36128.032.05
1168.5424,433.34123,310.33127.90191.03
1870.701,870.485,172.035.91316.60
Facility LME Variability (tons CO₂/MWh) Curtailment Regularity (hrs) Curtailment Magnitude (%) Emissions Ratio Threshold
100.147.7124.650.00240.9
110.234.2098.570.20.9
180.250.4299.830.360.85
Facility 10 — Georgia High Uptime · Low Avoided Emissions

Facility 10, located in Georgia, avoided 262.17 tons CO₂ with an uptime of 97.55%, LME variability of 0.14 tons CO₂/MWh, and maximum energy of 128.03 MWh. Only one curtailed event was detected that lasted 19 hours. The curtailment magnitude was 24.62%, indicating that the single curtailment event was shallow. The low LME variability, shallow curtailment, and poor alignment between energy use and LME over time (Pearson r = −0.03) are all suggestive of a large CDC with an operational strategy that maximizes uptime over emissions reduction or grid balancing. This facility is likely on a fixed price contract.

Facility 11 — Texas Low Uptime · High Avoided Emissions

Located in Texas, Facility 11 had an uptime of 68.54%, avoided 24,433.34 tons CO₂, had an LME variability of 0.23 tons CO₂/MWh, and maximum energy of 127.9 MWh—nearly identical capacity to Facility 10. Its LME-to-energy correlation was weakly inversely correlated (Pearson's r = −0.15), suggesting curtailment behavior is weakly influenced by the marginal generator's emissions. The curtailment magnitude was 98.57%, indicating deep—near complete—reduction of energy use. This facility is likely price responsive but the price signals are not well-aligned with the power generator's carbon intensity.

Facility 18 — Georgia Low Uptime · Highest NAE Per MWh

Facility 18 avoided 1,870.48 tons CO₂, had an uptime of 70.7%, LME variability of 0.25, and maximum energy of 5.91 MWh. On a per-MWh basis, this facility avoided the most emissions in our study—normalized avoided emissions of 316.6 tons CO₂/MWh with an emissions ratio of 0.36. The facility usually curtailed between 2pm–9pm daily, aligning with Georgia Power's summer on-peak hours (2pm–7pm). LME-energy correlation was moderate and inverse (Pearson's r = −0.45), suggesting curtailments are aligning with higher LME hours. This alignment between electricity prices and marginal emissions signals explains its per-megawatt-hour effectiveness.

§5 Discussion

This study presents the first large-scale empirical assessment of real-time load flexibility in cryptocurrency mining data centers. Our regression analysis found that uptime alone explained only 19% of the variance in avoided emissions. Incorporating locational marginal emissions (LME) variability and maximum energy increased the adjusted R² to 0.59. This aligns with theoretical expectations: avoided emissions scale with the product of energy curtailed and the relevant LME, but because LME values fluctuate by hour and region, uptime serves as a reliable predictor only when facilities are located in similar grid contexts and curtail during similar emissions conditions.

Our model confirms substantial variation in siting and operational behavior across CDCs. To reduce emissions effectively, operators should consider both regional marginal emissions and facility scale. When we normalized avoided emissions by maximum energy, deep curtailment and alignment with high-emission hours emerge as the most effective strategies, independent of facility size. These findings support the strategic siting of CDCs in locations where market incentives align with emission signals.

Our findings contrast with those of prior work that reported no significant emissions reduction from load flexibility due to their sample's baseline price responsiveness. Coordination between market signals and data center operations plays a critical role in emissions outcomes.

Limitations include inconsistencies in third-party metering, the constraint of summer months only, and the possibility that high-uptime facilities' uptimes may be slightly underestimated due to the curtailment detector treating some operational reductions as curtailment events.

§6 Conclusion

Our findings have direct implications for policy, operations, and climate mitigation strategies. CDCs—with a focus on Bitcoin mining—demonstrated in our analysis that even under different market structures, they are providing load flexibility to varying degrees and that there are opportunities for performance optimization. This load flexibility is due to the nature of the Bitcoin network's design—a marked departure from the traditional data center business model.

We found that when market structures aligned curtailment with marginal emissions, CDCs achieved the greatest emissions reductions. Policymakers and market designers that link incentive structures to real-time emissions can drive actionable results. Examples include dynamic pricing tied to locational marginal emissions and carbon-responsive demand programs that reward facilities for shifting load away from peak-emissions periods.

This study provides an empirical foundation for rethinking the sector's role in grid decarbonization, and calls for future energy modeling to carefully integrate these results. With clearer understanding of their load flexibility potential, constraints, and enabling conditions, this analysis lays the ground for making the most of the unique qualities of CDCs for a new class of climate solutions: ones that are fast, scalable, and rooted in behavioral and market-based shifts.
